title: Data centres are driving industry growth in Australia
gist: Australian GVA growth slowed to 0.3% q/q in March 2026 as Middle East conflict, inflation and rate hikes bite, but data centre investment across Sydney and Melbourne is the standout support for industry growth, spilling into utilities and professional services demand.

insights.claim: Australian GVA growth slowed to 0.3% q/q and 2.5% y/y in the March quarter, with output rising in 14 of 19 industries.
insights.speaker: Shaista Shaista
insights.so_what: Establishes a soft baseline; investors should weight resilient sectors (data centres, mining rebound) over the weak headline.
insights.quote: GVA rising 0.3% q/q and 2.5% y/y and output rising in 14 of 19 industries
insights.locator: Australian gross value added (GVA) growth slowed

insights.claim: Oxford Economics cut its 2026 Australian growth forecast to around 1.8%, down from a pre-conflict 2.4%, citing the Middle East conflict, above-target inflation and successive rate hikes.
insights.speaker: Shaista Shaista
insights.so_what: A ~60bp downgrade signals a materially weaker macro backdrop; position defensively on rate-sensitive Australian assets.
insights.quote: we now expect growth to ease to around 1.8% over 2026, down from a pre-conflict 2.4%

insights.claim: Data centre investment led by resilient business investment is the main support for Australian industry growth in 2026, with non-residential building activity set to surge as major facilities ramp up across Sydney and Melbourne.
insights.speaker: Shaista Shaista
insights.so_what: Data centre capex is the durable growth theme; watch construction, engineering and IT names exposed to the Sydney/Melbourne pipeline.
insights.quote: Non-residential building activity will continue to surge in 2026, with major facilities ramping up across Sydney and Melbourne.
insights.locator: Data centres are reshaping the investment landscape

insights.claim: Data centres will drive higher utilities demand as they account for a growing share of electricity consumption, and will boost professional services firms via engineering design and IT project work.
insights.speaker: Shaista Shaista
insights.so_what: Second-order beneficiaries: power generators/grid and engineering/IT consultancies tied to the data centre pipeline.
insights.quote: this translates into higher demand for utilities as data centres account for a growing share of electricity consumption
insights.locator: In time, this translates into higher demand for utilities

insights.claim: Combined with a wave of hospital construction, Australian business investment should remain sturdy despite the weaker macro environment.
insights.speaker: Shaista Shaista
insights.so_what: Investment resilience decouples from the soft macro; construction exposure may outperform the broad economy.
insights.quote: business investment should remain sturdy, despite the weaker macro environment
insights.locator: Combined with a wave of hospital construction

insights.claim: Cyclone Koji shut in thermal and coking coal across Queensland and New South Wales, pulling Australian mining down 1.5% q/q (the single largest drag) and transport down 1.3% q/q as port volumes fell.
insights.speaker: Shaista Shaista
insights.so_what: The Q1 mining weakness is weather-driven and temporary, not structural demand loss.
insights.quote: pulling mining down 1.5% q/q, the single largest drag on growth, and taking transport down 1.3% q/q as port volumes fell
insights.locator: Cyclone Koji was the quarter’s biggest swing factor

insights.claim: Little coal output was actually lost to Cyclone Koji as cargoes backed up onshore, so the June quarter should deliver a sharp rebound as delayed volumes clear.
insights.speaker: Shaista Shaista
insights.so_what: Expect a mechanical Q2 mining/transport bounce; the Q1 dip is a buying opportunity, not a trend.
insights.quote: the June quarter should bring a sharp rebound as the delayed volumes clear
insights.locator: Little of that output was lost, though

themes.theme: Middle East conflict and energy exposure

insights.claim: Australia imports more than 90% of its fuel, leaving the economy highly exposed to any fresh Middle East flare-up even though the Strait of Hormuz has reopened.
insights.speaker: Shaista Shaista
insights.so_what: Fuel-import dependence is a persistent tail risk; hedge Australian exposure against renewed energy price spikes.
insights.quote: with Australia importing more than 90% of its fuel, the economy remains exposed to any fresh flare-up
insights.locator: The Strait of Hormuz has reopened

insights.claim: The earlier spike in energy, fuel and fertiliser costs is still feeding through to inflation and squeezing margins for Australian manufacturers and diesel-reliant farmers.
insights.speaker: Shaista Shaista
insights.so_what: Margin pressure persists in manufacturing and agriculture even after prices normalise; watch input-cost-sensitive names.
insights.quote: still feeding through to inflation and squeezing margins for manufacturers and diesel-reliant farmers
insights.locator: The earlier spike in energy, fuel, and fertiliser costs

insights.claim: Australian mining export revenue has benefited from elevated commodity prices, but capacity-constrained LNG plants mean the gain shows up in earnings rather than physical output.
insights.speaker: Shaista Shaista
insights.so_what: LNG upside is a price/margin story, not a volume story; model earnings not production for exposed producers.
insights.quote: capacity-constrained LNG plants mean that shows up in earnings rather than output
insights.locator: Mining export revenue has benefited from elevated commodity prices
